prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 22-year-old Black man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I've had pain in my right lower belly since yesterday. It's a dull ache around 5/10. I feel a little nauseous but haven't vomited and I have less appetite. I had a stomach bug last week that's mostly better now.
 - History: No urinary burning. No prior surgeries.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 88, Temp 99.8F, SpO2 99%
 - Exam: Tender when they pressed the right lower belly, but no rebound and no guarding.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 12.4 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) HIGH
 - Urinalysis: Normal (ref Normal) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-belly pain with tenderness on exam plus an elevated white blood cell count can fit early appendicitis, even without vomiting or a high fever. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Your vitals are mostly reassuring and there’s no guarding/rebound, which can mean it’s early or less severe, but appendicitis can worsen quickly and needs imaging and a clinician’s exam to rule out. Recent “stomach bug” can also cause lingering inflammation or swollen lymph nodes, but with localized right-lower-quadrant pain and high WBC, appendicitis is a key concern.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (right-lower-belly pain, nausea, reduced appetite) plus tenderness on exam and a mildly elevated white blood cell count can fit early appendicitis. The lack of rebound/guarding and only a low-grade temperature do not rule it out—appendicitis can start subtly and worsen over hours. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis and some other causes of right-lower abdominal pain can become urgent if they progress, this should be evaluated promptly, often with repeat exam and possibly imaging (ultrasound/CT) rather than waiting to see if it passes.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (right-lower abdominal pain, nausea, reduced appetite) plus tenderness on exam and a mildly elevated white blood cell count can fit early appendicitis, even without rebound/guarding or a high fever. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis can worsen quickly and may start subtly, this combination needs prompt in-person reassessment and often imaging (usually ultrasound or CT) to rule it out. If pain worsens, becomes sharp, you develop vomiting, higher fever, or increasing abdominal tenderness, it becomes more urgent.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-belly pain with tenderness on exam plus a mildly elevated white blood cell count can fit early appendicitis, even without severe fever, vomiting, rebound, or guarding. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Recent “stomach bug” can also cause lingering inflammation or swollen intestinal lymph nodes that mimic appendicitis, but it’s hard to safely tell the difference without prompt in-person reassessment and possibly imaging. Because appendicitis can worsen quickly and becomes more dangerous if the appendix perforates, this combination of symptoms and labs shouldn’t be watched for weeks or only at home.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis and other abdominal problems can worsen quickly and may need imaging and treatment, this shouldn’t be watched at home.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-belly pain with tenderness on exam plus a mildly elevated white blood cell count can fit with early appendicitis, even without vomiting or a high fever. A normal urinalysis makes a urine infection or kidney stone less likely. The lack of rebound/guarding and near-normal vitals are somewhat reassuring, but appendicitis (and other causes like inflammation of the bowel) can start subtly and worsen over hours. Because appendicitis can become an emergency if it progresses, this combination of symptoms and labs warrants prompt in-person reassessment and likely imaging rather than waiting days.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-abdomen pain with tenderness on exam plus an elevated white blood cell count can mean your body is fighting inflammation or infection. One important cause in this location is appendicitis, which can start as a dull ache with mild nausea and low-grade fever and may not yet show rebound/guarding early on. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely, but it doesn’t rule out appendicitis or other bowel/inflammatory conditions. Because appendicitis can worsen and become an emergency, this combination of symptoms and labs should be evaluated urgently with an in-person exam and likely imaging.

One important cause is appendicitis, which can start as a dull ache with mild nausea and low-grade fever and may not yet show rebound/guarding early on. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely, but it doesn’t rule out appendicitis or other bowel/intestinal causes (including post-viral inflammation). Because appendicitis can worsen and sometimes perforate, this combination of symptoms and labs warrants urgent in-person evaluation and likely imaging.
